text:
[25] 
cAMP RECEPTOR IN Dictyostelium 
267 
based on antibodies are not currently able to identify exclusively the free 
(active) regulatory subunits, as can the biologically active direct cyto- 
chemical probe, described here. Chiefly this is because all currently avail- 
able antibodies recognize C and R subunits in the free as well as holoen- 
zyme form. Thus, they cannot be used to monitor net changes in the 
amount or location of free subunits upon stimulation of cells by agents 
that act via cAMP. This constraint for immunologic approaches may be 
unique to studies of the cAMP-dependent protein kinases since no other 
known regulatory enzyme requires dissociation of its subunits to become 
active. Antibodies to the protein kinases can, however, discriminate type 
I from type II R subunits, which the F : C probe cannot do. 
When prepared and used as described in this and the previous chapter 
the molecular probes for localizing R subunit and C subunit ~2,13 can yield 
data demonstrating protein kinase activities in individual cells. In con- 
junction with immunocytochemistry and conventional biochemistry this 
approach should be useful in studying cell regulatory events mediated by 
cAMP. 
[25] Identification and Ligand-Induced Modification of the 
cAMP Receptor in Dictyostelium 
By PETER KLEIN, ANNE THEIBERT, and PETER DEVREOTES 
I. Introduction 
Early in the development of Dictyostelium discoideum, an intercellu- 
lar communication system appears which coordinates the highly orga- 
nized aggregation of one million cells. Two responses essential to this 
coordinated aggregation are chemotaxis to cAMP and cAMP signaling 
(the synthesis and secretion of cAMP in response to extracellular cAMP), 
both of which are mediated by a cell surface cAMP receptor. These 
response systems provide excellent models for transmembrane signaling 
in eukaryotic cells. 
Most of the known components of these response systems are under 
tight developmental regulation. For example, receptor binding activity is 
low in growing cells, increases to a maximum of about 10 5 sites/cell at 5 to 
6 hr of development as the cells are spontaneously aggregating, and de- 
clines to a low level by 9 to 10 hr as multicellular structures assemble 
(Fig. 1).

FIG. 1. Expression of surface cAMP-binding sites during development: Cells were freed 
of nutrients (at t = 0) and starved in suspension as described in Methods. At the times 
indicated an aliquot was removed and the level of [3H]cAMP binding was determined. 
Photographs show the appearance of 4- and 7-hr cells plated on a monolayer. Territories in 
the 4-hr samples are 1-2 cm in diameter. Aggregates in 7-hr samples are 1-2 mm. 
Most of the cellular responses to cAMP subside within minutes when 
the stimulus concentration is held at a fixed level. This reversible adapta- 
tion to cAMP, which has been extensively studied for cAMP signaling (for 
a review, see Ref. I), could involve covalent modification of the cAMP 
receptor, which may be similar to mechanisms of adaptation postulated 
for other signal transduction systems such as methylation of bacterial 
chemotactic receptors and phosphorylation of rhodopsin in rod outer seg- 
ments. 
Using the photoreactive cAMP analog 8-N3-[32p]cAMP, the cAMP 
receptor has been identified as a doublet of Mr 40,000-43,000. 2 The dou- 
blet represents interconvertible bands that shift electrophoretic mobility 
in response to cAMP. 3 The shift in mobility, which is likely caused by 
covalent modification of the receptor, shows kinetics and cAMP dose 
P. Devreotes, in "The Development of Dictyostelium" (W. F. Loomis, ed.), p. 117. 
Academic Press, New York, 1982. 
2 A. Theibert, P. Klein, and P. Devreotes, J. Biol. Chem. 259, 12318 (1984). 
3 p. Klein, A. Theibert, D. Fontana, and P. Devreotes, J. Biol. Chem. 260, 1757 (1985). 
[25] 
cAMP RECEPTOR IN Dictyostelium 
269 
dependence that closely paralleled those observed for adaptation of the 
physiological responses. 4 Further, the shift to lower mobility is correlated 
with a 6-fold increase in phosphorylation of the receptor. 5 
This chapter focuses on methods to identify the cAMP receptor and to 
monitor the cAMP-induced modification by specific photoaffinity labeling 
and by in vivo phosphorylation. 
II. Photoaffinity Labeling with 8-Na-[32p]cAMP 
Photoaffinity labeling of nucleotide binding proteins in general and, 
specifically, cAMP-binding proteins (i.e., the regulatory subunit of 
cAMP-dependent protein kinases) has been described. 6,7 There are also 
several reports on photoaffinity labeling of cAMP-binding proteins in Dic- 
tyostelium with 8-N3-[32p]cAMP. 2,8-~° However, photoaffinity labeling of 
the surface cAMP receptor 2,9,m poses special problems: (1) the receptor- 
cAMP complex has a very rapid off rate which precludes washing the 
cells after ligand binding, thereby lowering the specificity of the photore- 
action; (2) there are several cAMP-binding proteins in Dictyostelium and, 
in particular, the regulatory subunit of cAMP-dependent protein kinase 
has a similar electrophoretic mobility (Mr 41,000) and is readily labeled 
with 8-N3-[32p]cAMp8; (3) Dictyostelium phosphodiesterase (PDE) avidly 
hydrolyzes 8-N3-cAMP; (4) cells contain abundant proteolytic activity 
and the cAMP receptor is extremely sensitive; (5) the receptor has a 
marked tendency to aggregate irreversibly. Methods to overcome each of 
these difficulties are discussed. 
The synthesis of 8-N3-cAMP has been described in detail elsewhere. 11 
The 32p analog is available from ICN on a monthly basis (specific activity 
= 60-70 Ci/mmol) and the 3H derivative is available from NEN (10-20 Ci/ 
mmol). As described in Ref. 6, the azido compound is stable up to 4 weeks 
if stored as the triethylammonium salt in methanol at -20 ° in the dark (as 
supplied by ICN). The compound is sensitive to higher temperatures and 
should be kept at -20 ° except for removal of sample. It is also reportedly

7 U. Walter and P. Greengard, this series, Vol. 99, p. 154. 
8 B. H. Leichtling, I. H. Majerfeld, E. Spitz, K. L. Schaller, C. Woffendin, S. Kakinuma, 
and H. V. Rickenberg, J. Biol. Chem. 259, 662 (1984). 
9 M. Juliani and C. Klein, J. Biol. Chem. 256, 613 (1981).

H B. E. Haley, this series, Vol. 46, p. 339.

CYCLIC NUCLEOTIDE ACTION 
[25] 
sensitive to acid. 6 The compound is stable to brief exposure to fluorescent 
room lights. To verify purity and photoreactivity of each new shipment, 
the compound should be analyzed by PEI thin-layer chromatography run 
with 2 N formic acid, pH 3.4, prior to and following extensive irradiation. 
If necessary, the azido cAMP can be further purified by ion-exchange 
chromatography on Dowex-50. 
III. Methods 
A. Preparation of Cells 
Development. Ax-3 cells of Dictyostelium discoideum are grown in 
400 ml of HL-5 medium ~2 to a density of 5 x 106/ml. To initiate develop- 
ment, cells are centrifuged (600 g, 5 min), resuspended in an equal volume 
of development buffer (DB) (5 mM Na2HPO4, 5 mM NaH2PO4, 2 mM 
MgSO4, 200/~M CaCI2), centrifuged again, resuspended at 2 x 107/ml in 
DB (100 ml), and shaken at 110-130 rpm in a l-liter Erlenmeyer flask. 
Spontaneous oscillations in cellular cAMP synthesis begin at 3.5 hr and 
continue through 7 hr. Early developmental markers, such as adenylate 
cyclase and surface cAMP receptors, reach peak expression (at least 12- 
fold above growth levels) within 5 to 6 hr (Fig. 1). 
B. Photoaffinity Labeling 
Cells are prepared at the appropriate time(s) in development, washed 
once in DB, once in DB with 10 mM DTT, and then resuspended in DB/ 
DTT at 108/ml at 0 ° (DTT is included to inhibit PDE). The 8-N3-[32p]cAMP 
(60-70 Ci/mmol) is dried from methanol under an N2 stream and resus- 
pended in DB/DTT at 0.75/zM. In a 12 x 75 mm glass test tube, I00/~1 of 
cells is added to 100/xl of 8-N3-[32p]cAMP. The N3-cAMP solution should 
be used immediately since DTT reduces the azido group. 5 In a parallel 
tube, nonradioactive cAMP is added at 100 tzM to determine nonspecific 
binding. The reaction is vortexed and after 30 sec, 3 ml of 98% saturated 
ammonium sulfate (AS) is added. 13 In the presence of AS, the half-time 
for dissociation of the receptor-cAMP complex is increased from a few 
seconds to several hours. After 5 min (to allow complete stabilization of 
binding), cells are centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min (Sorvall HS-4). The 
supernatant is discarded and the cells are washed again in AS. The cell 
pellet after the second AS wash is resuspended at a density of 108/ml in 
~z D. J. Watts and J. Ashworth, Biochem. J. 119, 171 (1970). 
13 p. Van Haastert and E. Kien, J. Biol. Chem. 258, 9636 (1983). 
[25] 
cAMP RECEPTOR ~N Dictyostelium 
271 
AS (200 /A). A small aliquot (10-20 /zl) is removed to measure non- 
covalent binding and the remainder is spotted on a plastic Petri dish 
at 4 °. The sample is irradiated with a short wave UV lamp (Mineral Light 
UVG-54, 254 nm) from a distance of 5 cm for 5 to 20 min. The cells are 
then transferred to receptor buffer (25 mM Tris, pH 7.5, 5 mM EDTA, 5 
mM DTT, with protease inhibitors described below) at a 10-fold dilution. 
The sudden decrease in osmotic strength causes cell lysis. The protease 
inhibitors (I mg/ml phenanthroline, 250/xg/ml CBZ-phenylalanine, 100 
/xg/ml PMSF, 1 mg/ml benzamidine, 10/zg/ml TPCK, 10/xg/ml TLCK, 
10/xg/ml TAME, 0.5/zg/ml antipain, 1 /xg/ml leupeptin, 0.5/ml aprotin, 
0.6/xg/ml chymostatin, 0.6/xg/ml pepstatin) are required to prevent ex- 
tensive degradation of the receptor. The lysate is centrifuged at 12,000 
rpm for 30 min (Sorvall SS-34); after centrifugation, no receptor is de- 
tected in the supernatant. The membrane pellet is solubilized in sample 
buffer (1.5% SDS, 62.5 mM Tris, pH 6.8, 5% glycerol, 2.5% DTT, and 
bromphenol blue) in a volume equal to the original reaction volume (200 
/zl). Membranes from l07 cells must be diluted into at least 200 /xl to 
prevent aggregation of the receptor. The samples are not boiled prior to 
electrophoresis since this also tends to aggregate the receptor. Once solu- 
bilized in SDS, the receptor is stable at room temperature for at least 3 
days and can be stored at -20 ° for several weeks. The samples are then 
electrophoresed on 10% SDS-PAGE, 14 stained with Coomassie blue, 
dried, and autoradiographed. A 50-/A sample usually requires no more 
than a 15- to 20-hr exposure to produce a dark band. 
IV. Specificity of Labeling 
A. Saturation Curve 
Intact cells are incubated with 8-N3-[32p]cAMP at concentrations rang- 
ing from 10 nM to 2 /xM and prepared for photolabeling as described 
above. Specific binding, plotted as a fraction of maximal binding, is shown 
in Fig. 2A (closed circles). Saturation occurs at approximately 1/xM. The 
KD determined by Scatchard analysis is 0.3 IzM. The affinity of the cAMP 
receptor for 8-N3-cAMP is about 10-fold lower than for cAMP (KD = 30- 
50 nM) but is higher than the affinity for other 8-substituted analogs. Cells 
are irradiated and membrane pellets analyzed by SDS-PAGE. About 90% 
of the covalently bound radioactivity migrates as a closely spaced doublet 
(Mr 40,000-43,000). The intensity of the doublet increases with increasing 
concentrations of 8-N3-[32p]cAMP, saturating at approximately 1 /xM

(Fig. 2B, lanes 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9, 11, 12, 14, 15). Addition of 100/xM cAMP to 
the binding assay completely inhibits photolabeling of the doublet (Fig. 
2B, lanes l, 4, 7, 10, 13). Quantification of scans of autoradiographs in 
Fig. 2B are plotted in Fig. 2A (open circles). The saturation curves for 
noncovalent binding and photoaffinity labeling of the doublet are iden- 
tical. 
Nonradioactive 8-N3-cAMP competes the binding of [3H]cAMP (inset, 
Fig. 2A) in the same concentration range that 8-N3-[32p]cAMP binds (K~ 
of about 1 /xM). These K~ values are within the reported concentration 
ranges over which cAMP (1 nM to 1/xM) and 8-N3-cAMP (approximately 
1 to 10 gM) stimulate chemotaxis and cAMP signaling. ~5 The total num- 
ber of sites (about 105/ceU) was the same when determined by [3H]cAMP 
or 8-N3-[32p]cAMP binding. In all of these determinations, the optical 
densities of the higher and lower mobility bands of the doublet were 
considered together. However, there is little apparent difference in the 
relative intensities of the two bands of the doublet at any level of satura- 
tion or competition. It was subsequently determined that the KD values 
for binding of [3H]cAMP to the higher and lower mobility forms are 33 and 
13 nM, respectively. 4 
B. Inhibition by cAMP 
Noncovalent binding of 8-Ns-[32p]cAMP is competed by cAMP in the 
10 nM to 1/xM range (Fig. 3A, closed circles), cAMP inhibits photolabel- 
ing in the same concentration range; quantification of scans of the autora- 
diographs are plotted in Fig. 3A (open circles). The cAMP competition 
curves for noncovalent binding and photoaffinity labeling are identical. 
The K~ for cAMP inhibition of both photolabeling and noncovalent bind- 
ing was about 70 nM (Fig. 3A), which is similar to the KD reported for 
[3H]cAMP binding (30-50 nM).

/zM. Binding was determined as described above and was normalized to maximal binding 
(closed circles). Maximal binding was typically about 105 sites/cell (80,000 cpm/10 v cells). 
(B) Cells were then irradiated and membranes were isolated and run on 10% SDS-PAGE. A 
representative autoradiogram is shown with ligand present at 20 nM (lanes I-3), 55.6 nM 
(lanes 4-6), 0.167 /xM (lanes 7-9), 0.5 /xM (lanes 10-12) and 1.5 /xM (lanes 13-15). The 
major band migrates as a doublet with an apparent molecular weight of 40,000 to 43,000. 
Lanes 1, 4, 7, 10, and 13 show photolabeling when 100 t~M cAMP was included in the 
binding assay. Autoradiograms were scanned and the optical density in the doublet (Mr 
40,000-43,000) was normalized as a fraction of maximal optical density [open circles in (A)]. 
Inset shows the competition of [3H]cAMP binding by 8-N3-cAMP.

FIG. 3. Inhibition by cAMP and analogs: (A) Cells were incubated with 8-Nr[32P]cAMP 
at 0.2 to 0.3/zM final concentrations with nonradioactive cAMP present at 10 nM, 0.1/zM, 
and 1/z M and binding was determined and plotted as described above (closed circles). Cells 
were irradiated and membranes were analyzed on SDS-PAGE as above. The autoradio- 
graphs were scanned and plotted (open circles) as the fraction of binding in the absence of 
cAMP. (B) Competition of photoaffinity labeling of the receptor (left) and the regulatory 
subunit of cAMP-dependent protein kinase (right). Photolabeling of the cAMP receptor was 
carried out as described in Methods. Photolabeling of the regulatory subunit of protein 
kinase was carried out as described in Ref. 7. cAMP, Nr-monobutyryl-cAMP, and 2'-H- 
cAMP were included in the labeling reactions at concentrations of 100, 40, and 1.0/xM, 
respectively. 
C. Analog Specificity 
The several cAMP-binding proteins in Dictyostelium can be distin- 
guished pharmacologically. For example, 2'-deoxy-cAMP has a high af- 
finity for the surface receptor but not for the regulatory subunit of cAMP- 
dependent protein kinase whereas N6-monobutyryl-cAMP has a high 
affinity for the regulatory subunit but does not bind to the surface receptor 
even at 0. l raM. Figure 3B illustrates competition with these two analogs 
of the photolabeling of the receptor and the regulatory subunit of the 
cAMP-dependent protein kinase. These differences in analog specificity 
have been used recently in studies of cAMP-mediated induction of gene 
expression during development. It appears that the surface receptor, 
[25] 
cAMP RECEPTOR IN Dictyostelium 
275 
rather than the regulatory subunit of the kinase, is the immediate target 
for cAMP effects on gene expression.~6 
V. Receptor Modification 
A. Electrophoretic Mobility Shift 
A reversible shift in electrophoretic mobility of the receptor has been 
shown to be stimulated by binding of extracellular cAMP. 3,4 The higher 
mobility from the receptor, found prior to cAMP stimulation, is desig- 
nated R (Mr 40,000) and a lower mobility form, found after cAMP stimula- 
tion, is designated D (Mr 43,000). In order to preferentially label either the 
R or D form of the receptor, aggregation stage cells are washed once in 
DB, resuspended at 108/ml at 22 °, and shaken at 150 rpm. To label the R 
form, cells are treated with 5 mM caffeine for 30 min, which blocks 
activation of adenylate cyclase. ~7 To label the D form, cells are treated 
with I0 mM DTT (to inhibit PDE) followed by stimulation with I0/zM 
cAMP for 13 min. Concanavalin A (100 /~g/ml) is then added (since it 
improves recovery of receptor) for 2 rain, then cells are washed in DB and 
then in DB/DTT at 0 °, resuspended at 2 x 108/ml in DB/DTT, and photo- 
labeled as described above. Frequently, the R form of the receptor ap- 
pears more intense than the D form from a parallel set of cells, even when 
the level of noncovalent binding of the photolabel is similar. This is appar- 
ently due to selective degradation of the D form of the receptor during 
membrane preparation and is also observed in Western blots. 
The concentration dependence and kinetics of receptor modification 
correlate closely with those of adaptation of several physiological re- 
sponses, including cAMP signaling, myosin phosphorylation, and cell 
shape change. The concentration dependence of the steady state distribu- 
tion of the R and D forms of the receptor is shown in Fig. 4A (closed 
circles). In cells pretreated with caffeine (so that the external cAMP con- 
centration is low), only 10% of receptors are found in the D form (Fig. 4A, 
lanes 1 and 2). A detectable increase in the fraction of receptors in the D 
form is observed with 0.5 nM cAMP; 50% of the maximal shift occurs 
with 30 nM cAMP; and the maximal fraction in the D form, about 80%, 
occurs with 1/zM cAMP. The concentration of cAMP that induces a half- 
maximal shift in the distribution of receptor forms is roughly equal to the 
Ko for cAMP binding. When the cAMP concentration is subsequently

FIG. 4. (A) Dose dependence of receptor modification: Cells were stimulated with the 
indicated concentrations of cAMP for 15 rain (closed circles), washed, and photolabeled as 
described above. The inset shows a portion of the gel (10% total acrylamide with 0.05% 
bisacrylamide). The first 2 lanes are controls and the remaining 14 lanes (from right to left) 
represent a 2-fold dilution series starting at 10 ~.M cAMP. After aliquots were taken at 15 
rain, 10 p.M cAMP was added to each set of cells. After an additional 15 min aliquots were 
taken for analysis (open circles). (B) In vivo phosphorylation of the receptor: Cells were 
labeled with [32p]phosphate for 30 min, divided, and incubated in the absence (-) or pres- 
ence (+) of l0/~M cAMP for 15 rain. Membranes were prepared and separated by SDS/ 
PAGE (10% total acrylamide with 0.05% bisacrylamide). The R and D forms (Mr 40,000- 
43,000) are apparent in the - and + lanes, respectively. 
increased from any concentration to 10 tzM and the incubation continued 
for 15 rain, the fraction of receptors in the D form rises to about 80% in all 
cases (Fig. 4A, open circles). 
The cAMP-triggered redistribution of receptors in each form occurs 
with a half-time of about 2 rain. By 15 min, a plateau value (80% in the D 
form) is reached which remains constant during persistent stimulation; 50 
nM cAMP, a concentration which occupies about 50% of the surface 
[25] 
cAMP RECEP'rog IN Dictyostelium 
277 
receptors, also induces a rapid redistribution which occurs with a half- 
time of about 2 min but the final plateau value reached is only 40% in the 
D form. When cells are washed free of cAMP and incubated at 22 °, there 
is a return toward the basal state which occurs with a half-time of 5-6 
min. This reversal is completely blocked at 0°. 4 
B. In Vivo Phosphorylation 
The shift to the D form is associated with a 6-fold increase in the level 
of phosphorylation of the receptor. 5 To phosphorylate the receptor in 
vivo, cells at the aggregation stage are washed twice in 10 mM MES, pH 
6.8, resuspended at 108/ml in 10 mM MES, and shaken at 150 rpm. Car- 
rier-free 32po 4 (1.0 mCi/ml of cells at 108/ml) is added for 30 min. For the 
R form, 5 mM caffeine is added at the same time. For the D form, DTT 
and cAMP are added 15 rain after addition of 32PO4. Con A is added and 
membranes are prepared as described above. When endogenous cAMP 
synthesis is suppressed, the R form of the receptor predominates in auto- 
radiographs of phosphorylated or photolabeled cells (Fig. 4B, lane -). 
After stimulation with cAMP, the D form predominates (Fig. 4B, lane +). 
C. Resolving R and D Forms by SDS/PAGE 
The resolution of the two forms of the receptor depends markedly on 
the concentration of bisacrylamide in the gel. Furthermore, the mobility 
of both forms of the receptor relative to other proteins (e.g., actin) also 
varies with the concentration of bisacrylamide. For example, using a 10% 
gel with 0.05% bis, the two forms of the receptor are widely separated 
(see Fig. 4A and B). The D form of the receptor migrates slower than actin 
while the R form migrates faster. Using a 10% gel with 0.25% bis the two 
forms migrate more closely together (see Fig. 2B and Fig. 3B) and both 
have a slightly higher mobility than actin. On a 10% gel with 1% bis, the 
two forms have very similar mobilities but a significantly higher mobility 
than actin. Due to this anomalous behavior, a gel with 1% bis resolves the 
receptor from contaminants that coelectrophorese on the 0.05% bis gels, 
and, in fact, two-dimensional preparative gels using two different bis 
concentrations are useful in purification of the receptor (manuscript in 
preparation). 
VI. Summary 
Photoaffinity labeling with 8-N3-[32p]cAMP and in vivo phosphory- 
lation with 32PO4 provide sensitive methods for the identification of the 
surface cAMP receptor. Photoaffinity labeling achieves high specificity 
278 
CYCLIC NUCLEOTIDE ACTION 
[26] 
while, in practice, the in vivo 32p labeling affords considerably higher 
sensitivity. Both methods allow fairly direct assays of cellular responses 
to an extracellular cAMP stimulus at the level of the receptor--a shift in 
electrophoretic mobility and a rise in phosphorylation. Quantification of 
the shift in mobility, assayed by photolabeting, has shown a strong corre- 
lation between modification of the receptor and adaptation. 4 These label- 
ing techniques will also help to elucidate the role of the concomitant 
changes in receptor phosphorylation, which may cause the change in 
electrophoretic mobility. Furthermore, photolabeling and 32p labeling will 
allow study of the possible multiple affinity classes of the cAMP recep- 
tor 18 and of the possible expression of cAMP receptors in later stages of 
development. 19

19 p. Schaap and W. Spek, Differentiation 27, 83 (1984). 
[26] Crystallizing Catabolite Gene Activator Protein with 
cAMP for Structural Analysis 
By IRENE T. WEBER 
The tertiary structure of a protein is essential for its biological func- 
tion. X-Ray crystallography is the only method currently available to 
determine the three-dimensional positions of the atoms in a protein mole- 
cule.~ The crystal structure provides valuable information on the interac- 
tion between the protein and ligands. Catabolite gene activator protein 
(CAP) from Escherichia coli has been crystallized in a complex with 
cAMP 2 and the structures of the wild type 3,4,19 and of a mutant, CAP91,2° 
have b~en determined.

York, 1976.
